summary.para: Replace SDL's memory allocation functions with a custom set.
It is not safe to call this function once any allocations have been made, as future calls to SDL_free will use the new allocator, even if they came from an SDL_malloc made with the old one!
If used, usually this needs to be the first call made into the SDL library, if not the very first thing done at program startup time.

member.param: custom malloc function.
custom calloc function.
custom realloc function.
custom free function.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but one should not replace the memory functions once any allocations are made!
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_MALLOC void * SDLCALL SDL_aligned_alloc(size_t alignment, size_t size);

summary.para: Allocate memory aligned to a specific alignment.
The memory returned by this function must be freed with , _not_ .
If alignment is less than the size of void *, it will be increased to match that.
The returned memory address will be a multiple of the alignment value, and the size of the memory allocated will be a multiple of the alignment value.

member.param: the alignment of the memory.
the size to allocate.
member.returns: a pointer to the aligned memory, or null if allocation failed.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SRand(System.UInt64)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_srand(Uint64 seed);
member.summary:
Seeds the pseudo-random number generator.
Reusing the seed number will cause Rand() to repeat the same stream of random numbers.
member.param: the value to use as a random number seed, or 0 to use .
member.threadsafety: This should be called on the same thread that calls Rand*()
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.Rand(System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Sint32 SDLCALL SDL_rand(Sint32 n);

summary.para: Generate a pseudo-random number less than n for positive n
The method used is faster and of better quality than rand() % n. Odds are roughly 99.9% even for n = 1 million. Evenness is better for smaller n, and much worse as n gets bigger.
Example: to simulate a d6 use Rand(6) + 1 The +1 converts 0..5 to 1..6
If you want to generate a pseudo-random number in the full range of Sint32, you should use: (int)RandBits()
If you want reproducible output, be sure to initialize with SDL_srand() first.

There are no guarantees as to the quality of the random sequence produced, and this should not be used for security (cryptography, passwords) or where money is on the line (loot-boxes, casinos). There are many random number libraries available with different characteristics and you should pick one of those to meet any serious needs.

member.param: the number of possible outcomes. n must be positive.
member.returns: a random value in the range of [0 .. n-1].
member.threadsafety: All calls should be made from a single thread
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RandF
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC float SDLCALL SDL_randf(void);

summary.para: Generate a uniform pseudo-random floating point number less than 1.0
If you want reproducible output, be sure to initialize with first.

There are no guarantees as to the quality of the random sequence produced, and this should not be used for security (cryptography, passwords) or where money is on the line (loot-boxes, casinos). There are many random number libraries available with different characteristics and you should pick one of those to meet any serious needs.

member.returns: a random value in the range of [0.0, 1.0).
member.threadsafety: All calls should be made from a single thread
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

There are no guarantees as to the quality of the random sequence produced, and this should not be used for security (cryptography, passwords) or where money is on the line (loot-boxes, casinos). There are many random number libraries available with different characteristics and you should pick one of those to meet any serious needs.

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RandR(System.UInt64@,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Sint32 SDLCALL SDL_rand_r(Uint64 *state, Sint32 n);

summary.para: Generate a pseudo-random number less than n for positive n
The method used is faster and of better quality than rand() % n. Odds are roughly 99.9% even for n = 1 million. Evenness is better for smaller n, and much worse as n gets bigger.
Example: to simulate a d6 use RandR(state, 6) + 1 The +1 converts 0..5 to 1..6
If you want to generate a pseudo-random number in the full range of Sint32, you should use: (int)RandBitsR(state)

There are no guarantees as to the quality of the random sequence produced, and this should not be used for security (cryptography, passwords) or where money is on the line (loot-boxes, casinos). There are many random number libraries available with different characteristics and you should pick one of those to meet any serious needs.

member.param: a pointer to the current random number state, this may not be null.
the number of possible outcomes. n must be positive.
member.returns: a random value in the range of [0 .. n-1].
member.threadsafety: This function is thread-safe, as long as the state pointer isn't shared between threads.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.WindowsMessageHook
member.code: typedef bool (SDLCALL *SDL_WindowsMessageHook)(void *userdata, MSG *msg);

summary.para: A callback to be used with .
This callback may modify the message, and should return true if the message should continue to be processed, or false to prevent further processing.
As this is processing a message directly from the Windows event loop, this callback should do the minimum required work and return quickly.

member.param: the app-defined pointer provided to .
a pointer to a Win32 event structure to process.
member.returns: true to let event continue on, false to drop it.
member.threadsafety: This may only be called (by SDL) from the thread handling the Windows event loop.
member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidJNIEnv
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void * SDLCALL SDL_GetAndroidJNIEnv(void);

summary.para: Get the Android Java Native Interface Environment of the current thread.
This is the JNIEnv one needs to access the Java virtual machine from native code, and is needed for many Android APIs to be usable from C.

The prototype of the function in SDL's code actually declare a void* return type, even if the implementation returns a pointer to a JNIEnv. The rationale being that the SDL headers can avoid including jni.h.

member.returns: a pointer to Java native interface object (JNIEnv) to which the current thread is attached, or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidActivity
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void * SDLCALL SDL_GetAndroidActivity(void);

summary.para: Retrieve the Java instance of the Android activity class.
The prototype of the function in SDL's code actually declares a void* return type, even if the implementation returns a jobject. The rationale being that the SDL headers can avoid including jni.h.
The jobject returned by the function is a local reference and must be released by the caller. See the PushLocalFrame() and PopLocalFrame() or DeleteLocalRef() functions of the Java native interface:
https://docs.oracle.com/javase/1.5.0/docs/guide/jni/spec/functions.html

member.returns: the jobject representing the instance of the Activity class of the Android application, or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidSDKVersion

summary.para: Query Android API level of the current device.

list.item: API level 35: Android 15 (VANILLA_ICE_CREAM)
API level 34: Android 14 (UPSIDE_DOWN_CAKE)
API level 33: Android 13 (TIRAMISU)
API level 32: Android 12L (S_V2)
API level 31: Android 12 (S)
API level 30: Android 11 (R)
API level 29: Android 10 (Q)
API level 28: Android 9 (P)
API level 27: Android 8.1 (O_MR1)
API level 26: Android 8.0 (O)
API level 25: Android 7.1 (N_MR1)
API level 24: Android 7.0 (N)
API level 23: Android 6.0 (M)
API level 22: Android 5.1 (LOLLIPOP_MR1)
API level 21: Android 5.0 (LOLLIPOP, L)
API level 20: Android 4.4W (KITKAT_WATCH)
API level 19: Android 4.4 (KITKAT)
API level 18: Android 4.3 (JELLY_BEAN_MR2)
API level 17: Android 4.2 (JELLY_BEAN_MR1)
API level 16: Android 4.1 (JELLY_BEAN)
API level 15: Android 4.0.3 (ICE_CREAM_SANDWICH_MR1)
API level 14: Android 4.0 (ICE_CREAM_SANDWICH)
API level 13: Android 3.2 (HONEYCOMB_MR2)
API level 12: Android 3.1 (HONEYCOMB_MR1)
API level 11: Android 3.0 (HONEYCOMB)
API level 10: Android 2.3.3 (GINGERBREAD_MR1)

member.returns: the Android API level.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

This path is unique to your application and cannot be written to by other applications.
Your internal storage path is typically: /data/data/your.app.package/files.
This is a C wrapper over android.content.Context.getFilesDir():
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/Context#getFilesDir()

member.returns: the path used for internal storage or null on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidExternalStorageState
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Uint32 SDLCALL SDL_GetAndroidExternalStorageState(void);

summary.para: Get the current state of external storage for this Android application.
The current state of external storage, a bitmask of these values: , .
If external storage is currently unavailable, this will return 0.

member.returns: the current state of external storage, or 0 if external storage is currently unavailable.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidExternalStoragePath
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const char * SDLCALL SDL_GetAndroidExternalStoragePath(void);

summary.para: Get the path used for external storage for this Android application.
This path is unique to your application, but is public and can be written to by other applications.
Your external storage path is typically: /storage/sdcard0/Android/data/your.app.package/files.
This is a C wrapper over android.content.Context.getExternalFilesDir():
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/Context#getExternalFilesDir()

member.returns: the path used for external storage for this application on success or null on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAndroidCachePath
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const char * SDLCALL SDL_GetAndroidCachePath(void);

summary.para: Get the path used for caching data for this Android application.
This path is unique to your application, but is public and can be written to by other applications.
Your cache path is typically: /data/data/your.app.package/cache/.
This is a C wrapper over android.content.Context.getCacheDir():
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/Context#getCacheDir()

member.returns: the path used for caches for this application on success or null on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RequestAndroidPermission(System.String,SDL3.SDL.RequestAndroidPermissionCallback,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_RequestAndroidPermission(const char *permission, SDL_RequestAndroidPermissionCallback cb, void *userdata);

summary.para: Request permissions at runtime, asynchronously.

This function never blocks. Instead, the app-supplied callback will be called when a decision has been made. This callback may happen on a different thread, and possibly much later, as it might wait on a user to respond to a system dialog. If permission has already been granted for a specific entitlement, the callback will still fire, probably on the current thread and before this function returns.

If the request submission fails, this function returns -1 and the callback will NOT be called, but this should only happen in catastrophic conditions, like memory running out. Normally there will be a yes or no to the request through the callback.
For the permission parameter, choose a value from here:
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/Manifest.permission

member.param: the permission to request.
the callback to trigger when the request has a response.
an app-controlled pointer that is passed to the callback.
member.returns: true if the request was submitted, false if there was an error submitting. The result of the request is only ever reported through the callback, not this return value.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.ShowAndroidToast(System.String,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_ShowAndroidToast(const char *message, int duration, int gravity, int xoffset, int yoffset);

summary.para: Shows an Android toast notification.
Toasts are a sort of lightweight notification that are unique to Android.
https://developer.android.com/guide/topics/ui/notifiers/toasts
Shows toast in UI thread.
For the gravity parameter, choose a value from here, or -1 if you don't have a preference:
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/Gravity

member.param: text message to be shown.
0=short, 1=long.
where the notification should appear on the screen.
set this parameter only when gravity >=0.
set this parameter only when gravity >=0.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SendAndroidMessage(System.UInt32,System.Int32)

summary.para: Send a user command to SDLActivity.
Override "boolean onUnhandledMessage(Message msg)" to handle the message.

member.param: user command that must be greater or equal to 0x8000.
user parameter.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.OnApplicationWillTerminate
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_OnApplicationWillTerminate(void);

summary.para: Let iOS apps with external event handling report onApplicationWillTerminate.

This functions allows iOS apps that have their own event handling to hook into SDL to generate SDL events. This maps directly to an iOS-specific event, but since it doesn't do anything iOS-specific internally, it is available on all platforms, in case it might be useful for some specific paradigm. Most apps do not need to use this directly; SDL's internal event code will handle all this for windows created by !

member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.IsTraySupported
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_IsTraySupported(void);

summary.para: Check whether or not tray icons can be created.
Note that this function does not guarantee that will or will not work; you should still check for errors.
Using tray icons require the video subsystem.

member.returns: true if trays are available, false otherwise.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread. It will return false if not called on the main thread.
member.returns: This function is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CreateTray(System.IntPtr,System.String)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Tray *SDLCALL SDL_CreateTray(SDL_Surface *icon, const char *tooltip);

summary.para: Create an icon to be placed in the operating system's tray, or equivalent.
Many platforms advise not using a system tray unless persistence is a necessary feature. Avoid needlessly creating a tray icon, as the user may feel like it clutters their interface.
Using tray icons require the video subsystem.

member.param: a surface to be used as icon. May be null.
a tooltip to be displayed when the mouse hovers the icon in UTF-8 encoding. Not supported on all platforms. May be null.
member.returns: The newly created system tray icon.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CreateTrayWithProperties(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Tray * SDLCALL SDL_CreateTrayWithProperties(SDL_PropertiesID props);

summary.para: Create an icon to be placed in the operating system's tray, or equivalent.
Many platforms advise not using a system tray unless persistence is a necessary feature. Avoid needlessly creating a tray icon, as the user may feel like it clutters their interface.
Using tray icons require the video subsystem.
These are the supported properties:

list.item: : an SDL_Surface to be used as the tray icon. May be null.
: a tooltip to be displayed when the mouse hovers the icon in UTF-8 encoding. Not supported on all platforms. May be null.
: an optional pointer to associate with the tray, which will be passed to click callbacks. May be null.
: an to be invoked when the tray icon is left-clicked. Not supported on all platforms. The callback should return true to show the default menu, or false to skip showing it. May be null.
: an to be invoked when the tray icon is right-clicked. Not supported on all platforms. The callback should return true to show the default menu, or false to skip showing it. May be null.
: an to be invoked when the tray icon is middle-clicked. Not supported on all platforms. May be null.
: an to be invoked when the tray icon is double-clicked. Not supported on all platforms. May be null.

member.param: the properties to use.
member.returns: The newly created system tray icon.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.6.0.

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_SetTrayIcon(SDL_Tray *tray, SDL_Surface *icon);
member.summary: Updates the system tray icon's icon.
member.param: the tray icon to be updated.
the new icon. May be null.
member.threadsafety: This function should be called on the thread that created the tray.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_TrayMenu *SDLCALL SDL_CreateTrayMenu(SDL_Tray *tray);

summary.para: Create a menu for a system tray.
This should be called at most once per tray icon.
This function does the same thing as , except that it takes a SDL_Tray instead of a SDL_TrayEntry.
A menu does not need to be destroyed; it will be destroyed with the tray.

member.param: the tray to bind the menu to.
member.returns: the newly created menu.
member.threadsafety: This function should be called on the thread that created the tray.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_TrayEntry *SDLCALL SDL_InsertTrayEntryAt(SDL_TrayMenu *menu, int pos, const char *label, SDL_TrayEntryFlags flags);

summary.para: Insert a tray entry at a given position.
If label is null, the entry will be a separator. Many functions won't work for an entry that is a separator.
An entry does not need to be destroyed; it will be destroyed with the tray.

member.param: the menu to append the entry to.
the desired position for the new entry. Entries at or following this place will be moved. If pos is -1, the entry is appended.
the text to be displayed on the entry, in UTF-8 encoding, or null for a separator.
a combination of flags, some of which are mandatory.
member.returns: the newly created entry, or null if pos is out of bounds.
member.threadsafety: This function should be called on the thread that created the tray.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetTrayEntryLabel(System.IntPtr,System.String)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_SetTrayEntryLabel(SDL_TrayEntry *entry, const char *label);

summary.para: Sets the label of an entry.

An entry cannot change between a separator and an ordinary entry; that is, it is not possible to set a non-NULL label on an entry that has a null label (separators), or to set a null label to an entry that has a non-NULL label. The function will silently fail if that happens.

member.param: the entry to be updated.
the new label for the entry in UTF-8 encoding.
member.threadsafety: This function should be called on the thread that created the tray.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

members.member:
extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_UpdateTrays(void);
Update the trays.
This is called automatically by the event loop and is only needed if you're using trays but aren't handling SDL events.
This function should only be called on the main thread. This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

Some of these flags are required; exactly one of them must be specified at the time a tray entry is created. Other flags are optional; zero or more of those can be OR'ed together with the required flag.

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.AudioStreamCallback
member.code: typedef void (SDLCALL *SDL_AudioStreamCallback)(void *userdata, SDL_AudioStream *stream, int additional_amount, int total_amount);

summary.para: A callback that fires when data passes through an SDL_AudioStream.
Apps can (optionally) register a callback with an audio stream that is called when data is added with , or requested with .

Two values are offered here: one is the amount of additional data needed to satisfy the immediate request (which might be zero if the stream already has enough data queued) and the other is the total amount being requested. In a Get call triggering a Put callback, these values can be different. In a Put call triggering a Get callback, these values are always the same.
Byte counts might be slightly overestimated due to buffering or resampling, and may change from call to call.

This callback is not required to do anything. Generally this is useful for adding/reading data on demand, and the app will often put/get data as appropriate, but the system goes on with the data currently available to it if this callback does nothing.

member.param: an opaque pointer provided by the app for their personal use.
the SDL audio stream associated with this callback.
the amount of data, in bytes, that is needed right now.
the total amount of data requested, in bytes, that is requested or available.
member.threadsafety: This callbacks may run from any thread, so if you need to protect shared data, you should use to serialize access; this lock will be held before your callback is called, so your callback does not need to manage the lock explicitly.
member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.AudioPostmixCallback
member.code: typedef void (SDLCALL *SDL_AudioPostmixCallback)(void *userdata, const SDL_AudioSpec *spec, float *buffer, int buflen);

summary.para: A callback that fires when data is about to be fed to an audio device.
This is useful for accessing the final mix, perhaps for writing a visualizer or applying a final effect to the audio data before playback.
This callback should run as quickly as possible and not block for any significant time, as this callback delays submission of data to the audio device, which can cause audio playback problems.

The postmix callback _must_ be able to handle any audio data format specified in spec, which can change between callbacks if the audio device changed. However, this only covers frequency and channel count; data is always provided here in SDL_AUDIO_F32 format.
The postmix callback runs _after_ logical device gain and audiostream gain have been applied, which is to say you can make the output data louder at this point than the gain settings would suggest.

member.param: a pointer provided by the app through , for its own use.
the current format of audio that is to be submitted to the audio device.
the buffer of audio samples to be submitted. The callback can inspect and/or modify this data.
the size of buffer in bytes.
member.threadsafety: This will run from a background thread owned by SDL. The application is responsible for locking resources the callback touches that need to be protected.
member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.AudioStreamDataCompleteCallback
member.code: typedef void (SDLCALL *SDL_AudioStreamDataCompleteCallback)(void *userdata, const void *buf, int buflen);

summary.para: A callback that fires for completed data.
When using to provide data to an SDL_AudioStream, it's not safe to dispose of the data until the stream has completely consumed it. Often times it's difficult to know exactly when this has happened.
This callback fires once when the stream no longer needs the buffer, allowing the app to easily free or reuse it.

member.param: an opaque pointer provided by the app for their personal use.
the size of buffer, in bytes, provided to .
the pointer provided to .
member.threadsafety: This callbacks may run from any thread, so if you need to protect shared data, you should use SDL_LockAudioStream to serialize access; this lock will be held before your callback is called, so your callback does not need to manage the lock explicitly.
member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetNumAudioDrivers
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetNumAudioDrivers(void);

summary.para: Use this function to get the number of built-in audio drivers.

This function returns a hardcoded number. This never returns a negative value; if there are no drivers compiled into this build of SDL, this function returns zero. The presence of a driver in this list does not mean it will function, it just means SDL is capable of interacting with that interface. For example, a build of SDL might have esound support, but if there's no esound server available, SDL's esound driver would fail if used.
By default, SDL tries all drivers, in its preferred order, until one is found to be usable.

member.returns: the number of built-in audio drivers.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioDriver(System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const char * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioDriver(int index);

summary.para: Use this function to get the name of a built in audio driver.

The list of audio drivers is given in the order that they are normally initialized by default; the drivers that seem more reasonable to choose first (as far as the SDL developers believe) are earlier in the list.
The names of drivers are all simple, low-ASCII identifiers, like "alsa", "coreaudio" or "wasapi". These never have Unicode characters, and are not meant to be proper names.

member.param: the index of the audio driver; the value ranges from 0 to - 1.
member.returns: the name of the audio driver at the requested index, or null if an invalid index was specified.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetCurrentAudioDriver

summary.para: Get the name of the current audio driver.
The names of drivers are all simple, low-ASCII identifiers, like "alsa", "coreaudio" or "wasapi". These never have Unicode characters, and are not meant to be proper names.

member.returns: the name of the current audio driver or null if no driver has been initialized.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioPlaybackDevices(System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_AudioDeviceID * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioPlaybackDevices(int *count);

summary.para: Get a list of currently-connected audio playback devices.

This returns of list of available devices that play sound, perhaps to speakers or headphones ("playback" devices). If you want devices that record audio, like a microphone ("recording" devices), use instead.
This only returns a list of physical devices; it will not have any device IDs returned by .
If this function returns null, to signify an error, count will be set to zero.

member.param: a pointer filled in with the number of devices returned, may be null.
member.returns: a 0 terminated array of device instance IDs or null on error; call for more information. This should be freed with when it is no longer needed.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

This returns of list of available devices that record audio, like a microphone ("recording" devices). If you want devices that play sound, perhaps to speakers or headphones ("playback" devices), use instead.
This only returns a list of physical devices; it will not have any device IDs returned by .
If this function returns null, to signify an error, count will be set to zero.

member.param: a pointer filled in with the number of devices returned, may be null.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioDeviceName(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const char * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioDeviceName(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid);
member.summary:
Get the human-readable name of a specific audio device.
WARNING: this function will work with and , returning the current default physical devices' names. However, as the default device may change at any time, it is likely better to show a generic name to the user, like "System default audio device" or perhaps "default [currently %s]". Do not store this name to disk to reidentify the device in a later run of the program, as the default might change in general, and the string will be the name of a specific device and not the abstract system default.
member.param: the instance ID of the device to query.
member.returns: the name of the audio device, or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioDeviceFormat(System.UInt32,SDL3.SDL.AudioSpec@,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioDeviceFormat(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, SDL_AudioSpec *spec, int *sample_frames);

summary.para: Get the current audio format of a specific audio device.

For an opened device, this will report the format the device is currently using. If the device isn't yet opened, this will report the device's preferred format (or a reasonable default if this can't be determined).
You may also specify or here, which is useful for getting a reasonable recommendation before opening the system-recommended default device.

You can also use this to request the current device buffer size. This is specified in sample frames and represents the amount of data SDL will feed to the physical hardware in each chunk. This can be converted to milliseconds of audio with the following equation:
Buffer size is only important if you need low-level control over the audio playback timing. Most apps do not need this.

member.param: the instance ID of the device to query.
on return, will be filled with device details.
pointer to store device buffer size, in sample frames. Can be null.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioDeviceChannelMap(System.UInt32,System.Int32@)
member.c: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioDeviceChannelMap(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, int *count);

summary.para: Get the current channel map of an audio device.
Channel maps are optional; most things do not need them, instead passing data in the [order that SDL expects](CategoryAudio#channel-layouts).
Audio devices usually have no remapping applied. This is represented by returning null, and does not signify an error.

member.param: the instance ID of the device to query.
On output, set to number of channels in the map. Can be null.
member.returns: an array of the current channel mapping, with as many elements as the current output spec's channels, or null if default. This should be freed with when it is no longer needed.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.OpenAudioDevice(System.UInt32,SDL3.SDL.AudioSpec@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_AudioDeviceID SDLCALL SDL_OpenAudioDevice(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, const SDL_AudioSpec *spec);

summary.para: Open a specific audio device.

You can open both playback and recording devices through this function. Playback devices will take data from bound audio streams, mix it, and send it to the hardware. Recording devices will feed any bound audio streams with a copy of any incoming data.

An opened audio device starts out with no audio streams bound. To start audio playing, bind a stream and supply audio data to it. Unlike SDL2, there is no audio callback; you only bind audio streams and make sure they have data flowing into them (however, you can simulate SDL2's semantics fairly closely by using instead of this function).

If you don't care about opening a specific device, pass a `devid` of either or . In this case, SDL will try to pick the most reasonable default, and may also switch between physical devices seamlessly later, if the most reasonable default changes during the lifetime of this opened device (user changed the default in the OS's system preferences, the default got unplugged so the system jumped to a new default, the user plugged in headphones on a mobile device, etc). Unless you have a good reason to choose a specific device, this is probably what you want.

You may request a specific format for the audio device, but there is no promise the device will honor that request for several reasons. As such, it's only meant to be a hint as to what data your app will provide. Audio streams will accept data in whatever format you specify and manage conversion for you as appropriate. can tell you the preferred format for the device before opening and the actual format the device is using after opening.

It's legal to open the same device ID more than once; each successful open will generate a new logical SDL_AudioDeviceID that is managed separately from others on the same physical device. This allows libraries to open a device separately from the main app and bind its own streams without conflicting.

It is also legal to open a device ID returned by a previous call to thi function; doing so just creates another logical device on the same physical device. This may be useful for making logical groupings of audio streams.
This function returns the opened device ID on success. This is a new, unique SDL_AudioDeviceID that represents a logical device.

Some backends might offer arbitrary devices (for example, a networked audio protocol that can connect to an arbitrary server). For these, as a change from SDL2, you should open a default device ID and use an SDL hint to specify the target if you care, or otherwise let the backend figure out a reasonable default. Most backends don't offer anything like this, and often this would be an end user setting an environment variable for their custom need, and not something an application should specifically manage.
When done with an audio device, possibly at the end of the app's life, one should call on the returned device id.

member.param: the device instance id to open, or or for the most reasonable default device.
the requested device configuration. Can be null to use reasonable defaults.
member.returns: the device ID on success or 0 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.OpenAudioDevice(System.UInt32,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_AudioDeviceID SDLCALL SDL_OpenAudioDevice(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, const SDL_AudioSpec *spec);

summary.para: Open a specific audio device.

You can open both playback and recording devices through this function. Playback devices will take data from bound audio streams, mix it, and send it to the hardware. Recording devices will feed any bound audio streams with a copy of any incoming data.

Unlike SDL2, there is no audio callback; you only bind audio streams and make sure they have data flowing into them (however, you can simulate SDL2's semantics fairly closely by using instead of this function).

If you don't care about opening a specific device, pass a `devid` of either or . In this case, SDL will try to pick the most reasonable default, and may also switch between physical devices seamlessly later, if the most reasonable default changes during the lifetime of this opened device (user changed the default in the OS's system preferences, the default got unplugged so the system jumped to a new default, the user plugged in headphones on a mobile device, etc). Unless you have a good reason to choose a specific device, this is probably what you want.

You may request a specific format for the audio device, but there is no promise the device will honor that request for several reasons. As such, it's only meant to be a hint as to what data your app will provide. This allows libraries to open a device separately from the main app and bind its own streams without conflicting.

It is also legal to open a device ID returned by a previous call to thi function; doing so just creates another logical device on the same physical device. This may be useful for making logical groupings of audio streams.
This function returns the opened device ID on success. This is a new, unique SDL_AudioDeviceID that represents a logical device.

member.param: the device instance id to open, or or for the most reasonable default device.
the requested device configuration. Can be null to use reasonable defaults.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.IsAudioDevicePhysical(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_IsAudioDevicePhysical(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid);

summary.para: Determine if an audio device is physical (instead of logical).

An SDL_AudioDeviceID that represents physical hardware is a physica device; there is one for each piece of hardware that SDL can see. Logical devices are created by calling or , and while each is associated with a physical device, there can be any number of logical devices on one physical device.

For the most part, logical and physical IDs are interchangeable--if you try to open a logical device, SDL understands to assign that effort to the underlying physical device, etc. However, it might be useful to know if an arbitrary device ID is physical or logical. This function reports which.
This function may return either true or false for invalid device IDs.

member.param: the device ID to query.
member.returns: true if devid is a physical device, false if it is logical.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.1.8.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.IsAudioDevicePlayback(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_IsAudioDevicePlayback(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid);

summary.para: Determine if an audio device is a playback device (instead of recording).
This function may return either true or false for invalid device IDs.

member.param: the device ID to query.
member.returns: true if devid is a playback device, false if it is recording.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PauseAudioDevice(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PauseAudioDevice(SDL_AudioDeviceID dev);

summary.para: Use this function to pause audio playback on a specified device.

This function pauses audio processing for a given device. Any bound audio streams will not progress, and no audio will be generated. Pausing one device does not prevent other unpaused devices from running.
Unlike in SDL2, audio devices start in an _unpaused_ state, since an app has to bind a stream before any audio will flow. Pausing a paused device is a legal no-op.
Pausing a device can be useful to halt all audio without unbinding all the audio streams. This might be useful while a game is paused, or a level is loading, etc.
Physical devices can not be paused or unpaused, only logical devices created through can be.

member.param: a device opened by .
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.ResumeAudioDevice(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_ResumeAudioDevice(SDL_AudioDeviceID dev);

summary.para: Use this function to unpause audio playback on a specified device.
This function unpauses audio processing for a given device that has previously been paused with . Once unpaused, any bound audio streams will begin to progress again, and audio can be generated.
Unlike in SDL2, audio devices start in an _unpaused_ state, since an app has to bind a stream before any audio will flow. Unpausing an unpaused device is a legal no-op.
Physical devices can not be paused or unpaused, only logical devices created through can be.

member.param: a device opened by .
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.AudioDevicePaused(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_AudioDevicePaused(SDL_AudioDeviceID dev);

summary.para: Use this function to query if an audio device is paused.
Unlike in SDL2, audio devices start in an _unpaused_ state, since an app has to bind a stream before any audio will flow.
Physical devices can not be paused or unpaused, only logical devices created through can be. Physical and invalid device IDs will report themselves as unpaused here.

member.param: a device opened by .
member.returns: true if device is valid and paused, false otherwise.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioDeviceGain(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC float SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioDeviceGain(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid);

summary.para: Get the gain of an audio device.
The gain of a device is its volume; a larger gain means a louder output, with a gain of zero being silence.
Audio devices default to a gain of 1.0f (no change in output).
Physical devices may not have their gain changed, only logical devices, and this function will always return -1.0f when used on physical devices.

member.param: the audio device to query.
member.returns: the gain of the device or -1.0f on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetAudioDeviceGain(System.UInt32,System.Single)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetAudioDeviceGain(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, float gain);

summary.para: Change the gain of an audio device.
The gain of a device is its volume; a larger gain means a louder output, with a gain of zero being silence.
Audio devices default to a gain of 1.0f (no change in output).

Physical devices may not have their gain changed, only logical devices, and this function will always return false when used on physical devices. While it might seem attractive to adjust several logical devices at once in this way, it would allow an app or library to interfere with another portion of the program's otherwise-isolated devices.

This is applied, along with any per-audiostream gain, during playback to the hardware, and can be continuously changed to create various effects. On recording devices, this will adjust the gain before passing the data into an audiostream; that recording audiostream can then adjust its gain further when outputting the data elsewhere, if it likes, but that second gain is not applied until the data leaves the audiostream again.

member.param: the audio device on which to change gain.
the gain. 1.0f is no change, 0.0f is silence.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CloseAudioDevice(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_CloseAudioDevice(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid);

summary.para: Close a previously-opened audio device.
The application should close open audio devices once they are no longer needed.
This function may block briefly while pending audio data is played by the hardware, so that applications don't drop the last buffer of data they supplied if terminating immediately afterwards.

member.param: an audio device id previously returned by .
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.BindAudioStreams(System.UInt32,System.IntPtr[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_BindAudioStreams(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, SDL_AudioStream * const *streams, int num_streams);

summary.para: Bind a list of audio streams to an audio device.

Audio data will flow through any bound streams. For a playback device, data for all bound streams will be mixed together and fed to the device. For a recording device, a copy of recorded data will be provided to each bound stream.

Audio streams can only be bound to an open device. This operation is atomic--all streams bound in the same call will start processing at the same time, so they can stay in sync. Also: either all streams will be bound or none of them will be.
It is an error to bind an already-bound stream; it must be explicitly unbound first.

Binding a stream to a device will set its output format for playback devices, and its input format for recording devices, so they match the device's settings. The caller is welcome to change the other end of the stream's format at any time with . If the other end of the stream's format has never been set (the audio stream was created with a NULL audio spec), this function will set it to match the device end's format.

member.param: an audio device to bind a stream to.
an array of audio streams to bind.
number streams listed in the streams array.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.BindAudioStream(System.UInt32,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_BindAudioStream(SDL_AudioDeviceID devid, SDL_AudioStream *stream);

summary.para: Bind a single audio stream to an audio device.
This is a convenience function, equivalent to calling BindAudioStreams(devid, stream, 1).

member.param: an audio device to bind a stream to.
an audio stream to bind to a device.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.UnbindAudioStreams(System.IntPtr[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_UnbindAudioStreams(SDL_AudioStream * const *streams, int num_streams);

summary.para: Unbind a list of audio streams from their audio devices.

The streams being unbound do not all have to be on the same device. All streams on the same device will be unbound atomically (data will stop flowing through all unbound streams on the same device at the same time).
Unbinding a stream that isn't bound to a device is a legal no-op.

member.param: an array of audio streams to unbind. Can be null or contain null.
number streams listed in the streams array.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

summary.para: Get the properties associated with an audio stream.
The application can hang any data it wants here, but the following properties are understood by SDL:

list.item: : if true (the default), the stream be automatically cleaned up when the audio subsystem quits. If set to false, the streams will persist beyond that. This property is ignored for streams created through , and will always be cleaned up. Streams that are not cleaned up will still be unbound from devices when the audio subsystem quits. This property was added in SDL 3.4.0.

where to store the input audio format; ignored if null.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetAudioStreamFormat(System.IntPtr,SDL3.SDL.AudioSpec@,SDL3.SDL.AudioSpec@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetAudioStreamFormat(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const SDL_AudioSpec *src_spec, const SDL_AudioSpec *dst_spec);

summary.para: Change the input and output formats of an audio stream.
Future calls to and and will reflect the new format, and future calls to must provide data in the new input formats.

Data that was previously queued in the stream will still be operated on i the format that was current when it was added, which is to say you can put the end of a sound file in one format to a stream, change formats for the next sound file, and start putting that new data while the previous sound file is still queued, and everything will still play back correctly.

If a stream is bound to a device, then the format of the side of the stream bound to a device cannot be changed (src_spec for recording devices, dst_spec for playback devices). Attempts to make a change to this side will be ignored, but this will not report an error. The other side's format can be changed.

member.param: the stream the format is being changed.
the new format of the audio input; if null, it is not changed.
the new format of the audio output; if null, it is not changed.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetAudioStreamFrequencyRatio(System.IntPtr,System.Single)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetAudioStreamFrequencyRatio(SDL_AudioStream *stream, float ratio);

summary.para: Change the frequency ratio of an audio stream.

The frequency ratio is used to adjust the rate at which input data is consumed. Changing this effectively modifies the speed and pitch of the audio. A value greater than 1.0f will play the audio faster, and at a higher pitch. A value less than 1.0f will play the audio slower, and at a lower pitch. 1.0f means play at normal speed.
This is applied during , and can be continuously changed to create various effects.

member.param: the stream on which the frequency ratio is being changed.
the frequency ratio. 1.0 is normal speed. Must be between 0.01 and 100.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioStreamInputChannelMap(System.IntPtr,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioStreamInputChannelMap(SDL_AudioStream *stream, int *count);

summary.para: Get the current input channel map of an audio stream.
Channel maps are optional; most things do not need them, instead passing data in the [order that SDL expects](CategoryAudio#channel-layouts).
Audio streams default to no remapping applied. This is represented by returning null, and does not signify an error.

member.param: the SDL_AudioStream to query.
On output, set to number of channels in the map. Can be null.
member.returns: an array of the current channel mapping, with as many elements as the current output spec's channels, or null if default. This should be freed with when it is no longer needed.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioStreamOutputChannelMap(System.IntPtr,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int * SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioStreamOutputChannelMap(SDL_AudioStream *stream, int *count);

summary.para: Get the current output channel map of an audio stream.
Channel maps are optional; most things do not need them, instead passing data in the [order that SDL expects](CategoryAudio#channel-layouts).
Audio streams default to no remapping applied. This is represented by returning null, and does not signify an error.

member.param: the SDL_AudioStream to query.
On output, set to number of channels in the map. Can be null.
member.returns: an array of the current channel mapping, with as many elements as the current output spec's channels, or null if default. This should be freed with when it is no longer needed.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetAudioStreamInputChannelMap(System.IntPtr,System.Int32[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetAudioStreamInputChannelMap(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const int *chmap, int count);

summary.para: Set the current input channel map of an audio stream.
Channel maps are optional; most things do not need them, instead passing data in the [order that SDL expects](CategoryAudio#channel-layouts).
The input channel map reorders data that is added to a stream via . Future calls to must provide data in the new channel order.

Each item in the array represents an input channel, and its value is the channel that it should be remapped to. To reverse a stereo signal's left and right values, you'd have an array of { 1, 0 }. It is legal to remap multiple channels to the same thing, so { 1, 1 } would duplicate the right channel to both channels of a stereo signal. An element in the channel map set to -1 instead of a valid channel will mute that channel, setting it to a silence value.
You cannot change the number of channels through a channel map, just reorder/mute them.

Data that was previously queued in the stream will still be operated on in the order that was current when it was added, which is to say you can put the end of a sound file in one order to a stream, change orders for the next sound file, and start putting that new data while the previous sound file is still queued, and everything will still play back correctly.
Audio streams default to no remapping applied. Passing a null channel map is legal, and turns off remapping.
SDL will copy the channel map; the caller does not have to save this array after this call.

If count is not equal to the current number of channels in the audio stream's format, this will fail. This is a safety measure to make sure a race condition hasn't changed the format while this call is setting the channel map.

Unlike attempting to change the stream's format, the input channel map on a stream bound to a recording device is permitted to change at any time; any data added to the stream from the device after this call will have the new mapping, but previously-added data will still have the prior mapping.

member.param: the SDL_AudioStream to change.
the new channel map, null to reset to default.
The number of channels in the map.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running. Don't change the stream's format to have a different number of channels from a different thread at the same time, though!
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetAudioStreamOutputChannelMap(System.IntPtr,System.Int32[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetAudioStreamOutputChannelMap(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const int *chmap, int count);

summary.para: Set the current output channel map of an audio stream.
Channel maps are optional; most things do not need them, instead passing data in the [order that SDL expects](CategoryAudio#channel-layouts).
The output channel map reorders data that is leaving a stream via .

Each item in the array represents an input channel, and its value is th channel that it should be remapped to. To reverse a stereo signal's left and right values, you'd have an array of { 1, 0 }. It is legal to remap multiple channels to the same thing, so { 1, 1 } would duplicate the right channel to both channels of a stereo signal. An element in the channel map set to -1 instead of a valid channel will mute that channel, setting it to a silence value.
You cannot change the number of channels through a channel map, just reorder/mute them.
The output channel map can be changed at any time, as output remapping is applied during .
Audio streams default to no remapping applied. Passing a null channel map is legal, and turns off remapping.
SDL will copy the channel map; the caller does not have to save this array after this call.

If count is not equal to the current number of channels in the audio stream's format, this will fail. This is a safety measure to make sure a race condition hasn't changed the format while this call is setting the channel map.

Unlike attempting to change the stream's format, the output channel map on a stream bound to a recording device is permitted to change at any time; any data added to the stream after this call will have the new mapping, but previously-added data will still have the prior mapping. When the channel map doesn't match the hardware's channel layout, SDL will convert the data before feeding it to the device for playback.

member.param: the SDL_AudioStream to change.
the new channel map, null to reset to default.
The number of channels in the map.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, as it holds a stream-specific mutex while running. Don't change the stream's format to have a different number of channels from a a different thread at the same time, though!
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PutAudioStreamData(System.IntPtr,System.IntPtr,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PutAudioStreamData(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const void *buf, int len);

summary.para: Add data to the stream.
This data must match the format/channels/samplerate specified in the latest call to , or the format specified when creating the stream if it hasn't been changed.

Note that this call simply copies the unconverted data for later. This is different than SDL2, where data was converted during the Put call and the Get call would just dequeue the previously-converted data.

member.param: the stream the audio data is being added to.
a pointer to the audio data to add.
the number of bytes to write to the stream.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but if the stream has a callback set, the caller might need to manage extra locking.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PutAudioStreamDataNoCopy(System.IntPtr,System.IntPtr,System.Int32,SDL3.SDL.AudioStreamDataCompleteCallback,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PutAudioStreamDataNoCopy(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const void *buf, int len, SDL_AudioStreamDataCompleteCallback callback, void *userdata);

summary.para: Add external data to an audio stream without copying it.

Unlike , this function does not make a copy of the provided data, instead storing the provided pointer. This means that the put operation does not need to allocate and copy the data, but the original data must remain available until the stream is done with it, either by being read from the stream in its entirety, or a call to or .
The data must match the format/channels/samplerate specified in the latest call to , or the format specified when creating the stream if it hasn't been changed.

An optional callback may be provided, which is called when the stream no longer needs the data. Once this callback fires, the stream will not access the data again. This callback will fire for any reason the data is no longer needed, including clearing or destroying the stream.

Note that there is still an allocation to store tracking information, so this function is more efficient for larger blocks of data. If you're planning to put a few samples at a time, it will be more efficient to use , which allocates and buffers in blocks.

member.param: the stream the audio data is being added to.
a pointer to the audio data to add.
the number of bytes to add to the stream.
the callback function to call when the data is no longer needed by the stream. May be NULL.
an opaque pointer provided to the callback for its own personal use.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but if the stream has a callback set, the caller might need to manage extra locking.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PutAudioStreamData(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PutAudioStreamData(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const void *buf, int len);

summary.para: Add data to the stream.
This data must match the format/channels/samplerate specified in the latest call to , or the format specified when creating the stream if it hasn't been changed.

Note that this call simply copies the unconverted data for later. This is different than SDL2, where data was converted during the Put call and the Get call would just dequeue the previously-converted data.

member.param: the stream the audio data is being added to.
a pointer to the audio data to add.
the number of bytes to write to the stream.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but if the stream has a callback set, the caller might need to manage extra locking.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PutAudioStreamPlanarData(System.IntPtr,System.IntPtr[],System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PutAudioStreamPlanarData(SDL_AudioStream *stream, const void * const *channel_buffers, int num_channels, int num_samples);

summary.para: Add data to the stream with each channel in a separate array.
This data must match the format/channels/samplerate specified in the latest call to SDL_SetAudioStreamFormat, or the format specified when creating the stream if it hasn't been changed.

The data will be interleaved and queued. Note that AudioStream only operates on interleaved data, so this is simply a convenience function for easily queueing data from sources that provide separate arrays. There is no equivalent function to retrieve planar data.

The arrays in channelBuffers are ordered as they are to be interleaved; the first array will be the first sample in the interleaved data. Any individual array may be null; in this case, silence will be interleaved for that channel.

numChannels specifies how many arrays are in channelBuffers. This can be used as a safety to prevent overflow, in case the stream format has changed elsewhere. If more channels are specified than the current input spec, they are ignored. If less channels are specified, the missing arrays are treated as if they are null (silence is written to those channels). If the count is -1, SDL will assume the array count matches the current input spec.

Note that numSamples is the number of _samples per array_. This can also be thought of as the number of _sample frames_ to be queued. A value of 1 with stereo arrays will queue two samples to the stream. This is different than PutAudioStreamData, which wants the size of a single array in bytes.

member.param: the stream the audio data is being added to.
a pointer to an array of arrays, one array per channel.
the number of arrays in channelBuffers or -1.
the number of _samples_ per array to write to the stream.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but if the stream has a callback set, the caller might need to manage extra locking.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioStreamData(System.IntPtr,System.IntPtr,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioStreamData(SDL_AudioStream *stream, void *buf, int len);

summary.para: Get converted/resampled data from the stream.
The input/output data format/channels/samplerate is specified when creating the stream, and can be changed after creation by calling .

Note that any conversion and resampling necessary is done during this call, and SDL_PutAudioStreamData simply queues unconverted data for later. This is different than SDL2, where that work was done while inputting new data to the stream and requesting the output just copied the converted data.

member.param: the stream the audio is being requested from.
a buffer to fill with audio data.
the maximum number of bytes to fill.
member.returns: the number of bytes read from the stream or -1 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread, but if the stream has a callback set, the caller might need to manage extra locking.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetAudioStreamData(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetAudioStreamData(SDL_AudioStream *stream, void *buf, int len);

summary.para: Get converted/resampled data from the stream.
The input/output data format/channels/samplerate is specified when creating the stream, and can be changed after creation by calling .

Note that any conversion and resampling necessary is done during this call, and SDL_PutAudioStreamData simply queues unconverted data for later. This is different than SDL2, where that work was done while inputting new data to the stream and requesting the output just copied the converted data.

member.param: the stream the audio is being requested from.
a buffer to fill with audio data.
the maximum number of bytes to fill.
member.returns: the number of bytes read from the stream or -1 on failure; call for more information.
