member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PumpEvents
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_PumpEvents(void);

summary.para: Pump the event loop, gathering events from the input devices.
This function updates the event queue and internal input device state.

gathers all the pending input information from devices and places it in the event queue. Without calls to no events would ever be placed on the queue. Often the need for calls to is hidden from the user since and implicitly call . However, if you are not polling or waiting for events (e.g. you are filtering them), then you must call to force an event queue update.

member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PeepEvents(System.IntPtr,System.Int32,SDL3.SDL.EventAction,System.UInt32,System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_PeepEvents(SDL_Event *events, int numevents, SDL_EventAction action, Uint32 minType, Uint32 maxType);

summary.para: Check the event queue for messages and optionally return them.
action may be any of the following:

list.item: : up to numevents events will be added to the back of the event queue.
: numevents events at the front of the event queue, within the specified minimum and maximum type, will be returned to the caller and will not be removed from the queue. If you pass null for
events, then numevents is ignored and the total number of matching events will be returned.
: up to numevents events at the front of the event queue, within the specified minimum and maximum type, will be returned to the caller and will be removed from the queue.

summary.para: You may have to call before calling this function. Otherwise, the events may not be ready to be filtered when you call .

member.param: destination buffer for the retrieved events, may be null to leave the events in the queue and return the number of events that would have been stored.
if action is , the number of events to add back to the event queue; if action is or , the maximum number of events to retrieve.
action to take; see Remarks for details.
minimum value of the event type to be considered; is a safe choice.
maximum value of the event type to be considered; is a safe choice.
member.returns: the number of events actually stored or -1 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.FlushEvent(System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_FlushEvent(Uint32 type);

summary.para: Clear events of a specific type from the event queue.
This will unconditionally remove any events from the queue that match type. If you need to remove a range of event types, use instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this function.
This function only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call on the main thread immediately before the flush call.
If you have user events with custom data that needs to be freed, you should use to remove and clean up those events before calling this function.

member.param: the type of event to be cleared; see for details.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.FlushEvents(System.UInt32,System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_FlushEvents(Uint32 minType, Uint32 maxType);

summary.para: Clear events of a range of types from the event queue.
This will unconditionally remove any events from the queue that are in the range of minType to maxType, inclusive. If you need to remove a single event type, use instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this function.
This function only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call on the main thread immediately before the flush call.

member.param: the low end of event type to be cleared, inclusive; see for details.
the high end of event type to be cleared, inclusive; see for details.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PollEvent(SDL3.SDL.Event@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PollEvent(SDL_Event *event);

summary.para: Poll for currently pending events.
If event is not null, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the structure pointed to by event.
If event is null, it simply returns true if there is an event in the queue, but will not remove it from the queue.
As this function may implicitly call , you can only call this function in the thread that initialized the video subsystem.
is the favored way of receiving system events since it can be done from the main loop and does not suspend the main loop while waiting on an event to be posted.
The common practice is to fully process the event queue once every frame, usually as a first step before updating the game's state:

Note that Windows (and possibly other platforms) has a quirk about how it handles events while dragging/resizing a window, which can cause this function to block for significant amounts of time. Technical explanations and solutions are discussed on the wiki:
https://wiki.libsdl.org/SDL3/AppFreezeDuringDrag

member.param: the structure to be filled with the next event from the queue, or null.
member.returns: true if this got an event or false if there are none available.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PollEvent(System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PollEvent(SDL_Event *event);

summary.para: Poll for currently pending events.

If event is not null, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the SDL_Event structure pointed to by `event`. The 1 returned refers to this event, immediately stored in the SDL Event structure -- not an event to follow.
As this function may implicitly call , you can only call this function in the thread that set the video mode.
is the favored way of receiving system events since it can be done from the main loop and does not suspend the main loop while waiting on an event to be posted.
The common practice is to fully process the event queue once every frame, usually as a first step before updating the game's state:

Note that Windows (and possibly other platforms) has a quirk about how it handles events while dragging/resizing a window, which can cause this function to block for significant amounts of time. Technical explanations and solutions are discussed on the wiki:
https://wiki.libsdl.org/SDL3/AppFreezeDuringDrag

member.param: the structure to be filled with the next event from the queue, or null.
member.returns: true if this got an event or false if there are none available.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.WaitEvent(SDL3.SDL.Event@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_WaitEvent(SDL_Event *event);

summary.para: Wait indefinitely for the next available event.
If event is not null, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the structure pointed to by event.
As this function may implicitly call , you can only call this function in the thread that initialized the video subsystem.

member.param: the structure to be filled in with the next event from the queue, or null.
member.returns: true on success or false if there was an error while waiting for events; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.WaitEventTimeout(SDL3.SDL.Event@,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_WaitEventTimeout(SDL_Event *event, Sint32 timeoutMS);

summary.para: Wait until the specified timeout (in milliseconds) for the next available event.
If event is not null, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the structure pointed to by event.
As this function may implicitly call , you can only call this function in the thread that initialized the video subsystem.
The timeout is not guaranteed, the actual wait time could be longer due to system scheduling.

member.param: the structure to be filled in with the next event from the queue, or null.
the maximum number of milliseconds to wait for the next available event.
member.returns: true if this got an event or false if the timeout elapsed without any events available.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.PushEvent(SDL3.SDL.Event@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_PushEvent(SDL_Event *event);

summary.para: Add an event to the event queue.

The event queue can actually be used as a two way communication channel. Not only can events be read from the queue, but the user can also push their own events onto it. event is a pointer to the event structure you wish to push onto the queue. The event is copied into the queue, and the caller may dispose of the memory pointed to after returns.
Note: Pushing device input events onto the queue doesn't modify the state of the device within SDL.
Note: Events pushed onto the queue with get passed through the event filter but events added with do not.
For pushing application-specific events, please use to get an event type that does not conflict with other code that also wants its own custom event types.

member.param: the to be added to the queue.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetEventFilter(SDL3.SDL.EventFilter,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_SetEventFilter(SDL_EventFilter filter, void *userdata);

summary.para: Set up a filter to process all events before they are added to the internal event queue.
If you just want to see events without modifying them or preventing them from being queued, you should use instead.

If the filter function returns true when called, then the event will be added to the internal queue. If it returns false, then the event will be dropped from the queue, but the internal state will still be updated. This allows selective filtering of dynamically arriving events.

WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event filter function, as it may run in a different thread! The exception is handling of , which is guaranteed to be sent from the OS on the main thread and you are expected to redraw your window in response to this event.
On platforms that support it, if the quit event is generated by an interrupt sinal (e.g. pressing Ctrl-C), it will be delivered to the application at the next event poll.

There is one caveat when dealing with the event type. The event filter is only called when the window manager desires to close the application window. If the event filter returns 1, then the window will be closed, otherwise the window will remain open if possible.
Note: Disabled events never make it to the event filter function; see .
Note: If you just want to inspect events without filtering, you should use instead.
Note: Events pushed onto the queue with get passed through the event filter, but events pushed onto the queue with do not.

member.param: a function to call when an event happens.
a pointer that is passed to filter.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.AddEventWatch(SDL3.SDL.EventFilter,System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_AddEventWatch(SDL_EventFilter filter, void *userdata);

summary.para: Add a callback to be triggered when an event is added to the event queue.
filter will be called when an event happens, and its return value is ignored.
WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event filter function, as it may run in a different thread!
If the quit event is generated by a signal (e.g. SIGINT), it will bypass the internal queue and be delivered to the watch callback immediately, and arrive at the next event poll.
Note: the callback is called for events posted by the user through , but not for disabled events, nor for events by a filter callback set with , nor for events posted by the user through .

member.param: an function to call when an event happens.
a pointer that is passed to filter.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetEventDescription(SDL3.SDL.Event@,System.Byte[],System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetEventDescription(const SDL_Event *event, char *buf, int buflen);

summary.para: Generate a human-readable description of an event.
This will fill buf with a null-terminated string that might look something like this:
summary.code: EventMouseMotion(timestamp=1140256324 windowid=2 which=0 state=0 x=492.99 y=139.09 xrel=52 yrel=6)
summary.para: The exact format of the string is not guaranteed; it is intended for logging purposes, to be read by a human, and not parsed by a computer.

The returned value follows the same rules as SDL_snprintf(): buf will always be NULL-terminated (unless buflen is zero), and will be truncated if buflen is too small. The return code is the number of bytes needed for the complete string, not counting the NULL-terminator, whether the string was truncated or not. Unlike SDL_snprintf(), though, this function never returns -1.

member.param: an event to describe. May be NULL.
the buffer to fill with the description string. May be NULL.
the maximum bytes that can be written to buf.
member.returns: number of bytes needed for the full string, not counting the null-terminator byte.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.TouchFingerEvent

summary.para: Touch finger event structure (event.tfinger.*)

Coordinates in this event are normalized. x and y are normalized to a range between 0.0f and 1.0f, relative to the window, so (0,0) is the top left and (1,1) is the bottom right. Delta coordinates dx and dy are normalized in the ranges of -1.0f (traversed all the way from the bottom or right to all the way up or left) to 1.0f (traversed all the way from the top or left to all the way down or right).

Note that while the coordinates are _normalized_, they are not _clamped_, which means in some circumstances you can get a value outside of this range. For example, a renderer using logical presentation might give a negative value when the touch is in the letterboxing. Some platforms might report a touch outside of the window, which will also be outside of the range.

member.since: This struct is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.GamepadAxis

summary.para: The list of axes available on a gamepad
Thumbstick axis values range from to , and are centered within ~8000 of zero, though advanced UI will allow users to set or autodetect the dead zone, which varies between gamepads.
Trigger axis values range from 0 (released) to (fully pressed) when reported by . Note that this is not the same range that will be reported by the lower-level .

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.GamepadBinding

summary.para: A mapping between one joystick input to a gamepad control.
A gamepad has a collection of several bindings, to say, for example, when joystick button number 5 is pressed, that should be treated like the gamepad's "start" button.

SDL has these bindings built-in for many popular controllers, and can add more with a simple text string. Those strings are parsed into a collection of these structs to make it easier to operate on the data.

member.since: This struct is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.GamepadBindingType

summary.para: Types of gamepad control bindings.

A gamepad is a collection of bindings that map arbitrary joystick buttons, axes and hat switches to specific positions on a generic console-style gamepad. This enum is used as part of to specify those mappings.

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.GamepadButton

summary.para: The list of buttons available on a gamepad

For controllers that use a diamond pattern for the face buttons, the south/east/west/north buttons below correspond to the locations in the diamond pattern. For Xbox controllers, this would be A/B/X/Y, for Nintendo Switch controllers, this would be B/A/Y/X, for GameCube controllers this would be A/X/B/Y, for PlayStation controllers this would be Cross/Circle/Square/Triangle.

For controllers that don't use a diamond pattern for the face buttons, the south/east/west/north buttons indicate the buttons labeled A, B, C, D, or 1, 2, 3, 4, or for controllers that aren't labeled, they are the primary, secondary, etc. buttons.

The activate action is often the south button and the cancel action is often the east button, but in some regions this is reversed, so your game should allow remapping actions based on user preferences.
You can query the labels for the face buttons using

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

summary.para: The set of gamepad button labels
This isn't a complete set, just the face buttons to make it easy to show button prompts.
For a complete set, you should look at the button and gamepad type and have a set of symbols that work well with your art style.

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

summary.para: Standard gamepad types.

This type does not necessarily map to first-party controllers from Microsoft/Sony/Nintendo; in many cases, third-party controllers can report as these, either because they were designed for a specific console, or they simply most closely match that console's controllers (does it have A/B/X/Y buttons or X/O/Square/Triangle? Does it have a touchpad? etc).

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.AddGamepadMapping(System.String)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_AddGamepadMapping(const char *mapping);

summary.para: Add support for gamepads that SDL is unaware of or change the binding of an existing gamepad.

The mapping string has the format "GUID,name,mapping", where GUID is the string value from , name is the human readable string for the device and mappings are gamepad mappings to joystick ones. Under Windows there is a reserved GUID of "xinput" that covers all XInput devices. The mapping format for joystick is:

list.item: bX: a joystick button, index X
hX.Y: hat X with value Y
aX: axis X of the joystick

summary.para: Buttons can be used as a gamepad axes and vice versa.
If a device with this GUID is already plugged in, SDL will generate an event.
This string shows an example of a valid mapping for a gamepad:

member.param: the mapping string.
member.returns: 1 if a new mapping is added, 0 if an existing mapping is updated, -1 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.AddGamepadMappingsFromIO(System.IntPtr,System.Boolean)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_AddGamepadMappingsFromIO(SDL_IOStream *src, bool closeio);

summary.para: Load a set of gamepad mappings from an SDL_IOStream.
You can call this function several times, if needed, to load different database files.
If a new mapping is loaded for an already known gamepad GUID, the later version will overwrite the one currently loaded.
Any new mappings for already plugged in controllers will generate events.
Mappings not belonging to the current platform or with no platform field specified will be ignored (i.e. mappings for Linux will be ignored in Windows, etc).
This function will load the text database entirely in memory before processing it, so take this into consideration if you are in a memory constrained environment.

member.param: the data stream for the mappings to be added.
if true, calls on src before returning, even in the case of an error.
member.returns: the number of mappings added or -1 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

These properties are shared with the underlying joystick object.
The following read-only properties are provided by SDL:

list.item: : true if this gamepad has an LED that has adjustable brightness
: true if this gamepad has an LED that has adjustable color
: true if this gamepad has a player LED
: true if this gamepad has left/right rumble
: true if this gamepad has simple trigger rumble

member.param: a gamepad identifier previously returned by .
member.returns: a valid property ID on success or 0 on failure; call It is safe to call this function from any thread. for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetGamepadPowerInfo(System.IntPtr,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_PowerState SDLCALL SDL_GetGamepadPowerInfo(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad, int *percent);

summary.para: Get the battery state of a gamepad.

You should never take a battery status as absolute truth. Batteries (especially failing batteries) are delicate hardware, and the values reported here are best estimates based on what that hardware reports. It's not uncommon for older batteries to lose stored power much faster than it reports, or completely drain when reporting it has 20 percent left, etc.

member.param: the gamepad object to query.
a pointer filled in with the percentage of battery life left, between 0 and 100, or null to ignore. This will be filled in with -1 we can't determine a value or there is no battery.
member.returns: the current battery state.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetGamepadJoystick(System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Joystick * SDLCALL SDL_GetGamepadJoystick(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad);

summary.para: Get the underlying joystick from a gamepad.

This function will give you a SDL_Joystick object, which allows you to use the SDL_Joystick functions with a SDL_Gamepad object. This would be useful for getting a joystick's position at any given time, even if it hasn't moved (moving it would produce an event, which would have the axis' value).
The pointer returned is owned by the SDL_Gamepad. You should not call on it, for example, since doing so will likely cause SDL to crash.

member.param: the gamepad object that you want to get a joystick from.
member.returns: an SDL_Joystick object, or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_GamepadHasAxis(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad, SDL_GamepadAxis axis);

summary.para: Query whether a gamepad has a given axis.
This merely reports whether the gamepad's mapping defined this axis, as that is all the information SDL has about the physical device.

member.param: a gamepad.
an axis enum value (an value).
member.returns: true if the gamepad has this axis, false otherwise.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetGamepadAxis(System.IntPtr,SDL3.SDL.GamepadAxis)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Sint16 SDLCALL SDL_GetGamepadAxis(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad, SDL_GamepadAxis axis);

summary.para: Get the current state of an axis control on a gamepad.
The axis indices start at index 0.
For thumbsticks, the state is a value ranging from -32768 (up/left) to 32767 (down/right).

Triggers range from 0 when released to 32767 when fully pressed, and never return a negative value. Note that this differs from the value reported by the lower-level , which normally uses the full range.
Note that for invalid gamepads or axes, this will return 0. Zero is also a valid value in normal operation; usually it means a centered axis.

member.param: a gamepad.
an axis index (one of the values).
member.returns: axis state.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RumbleGamepad(System.IntPtr,System.UInt16,System.UInt16,System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_RumbleGamepad(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad, Uint16 low_frequency_rumble, Uint16 high_frequency_rumble, Uint32 duration_ms);

summary.para: Start a rumble effect on a gamepad.
Each call to this function cancels any previous rumble effect, and calling it with 0 intensity stops any rumbling.
This function requires you to process SDL events or call to update rumble state.

member.param: the gamepad to vibrate.
the intensity of the low frequency (left) rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the intensity of the high frequency (right) rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the duration of the rumble effect, in milliseconds.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RumbleGamepadTriggers(System.IntPtr,System.UInt16,System.UInt16,System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_RumbleGamepadTriggers(SDL_Gamepad *gamepad, Uint16 left_rumble, Uint16 right_rumble, Uint32 duration_ms);

summary.para: Start a rumble effect in the gamepad's triggers.
Each call to this function cancels any previous trigger rumble effect, and calling it with 0 intensity stops any rumbling.
Note that this is rumbling of the _triggers_ and not the gamepad as a whole. This is currently only supported on Xbox One gamepads. If you want the (more common) whole-gamepad rumble, use instead.
This function requires you to process SDL events or call to update rumble state.

member.param: the gamepad to vibrate.
the intensity of the left trigger rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the intensity of the right trigger rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the duration of the rumble effect, in milliseconds.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDInit
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_init(void);

summary.para: Initialize the HIDAPI library.

This function initializes the HIDAPI library. Calling it is not strictly necessary, as it will be called automatically by , , and if needed. This function should be called at the beginning of execution however, if there is a chance of HIDAPI handles being opened by different threads simultaneously.
Each call to this function should have a matching call to

member.returns: 0 on success or a negative error code on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDExit
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_exit(void);

summary.para: Finalize the HIDAPI library.
This function frees all of the static data associated with HIDAPI. It should be called at the end of execution to avoid memory leaks.

member.returns: 0 on success or a negative error code on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDDeviceChangeCount
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Uint32 SDLCALL SDL_hid_device_change_count(void);

summary.para: Check to see if devices may have been added or removed.

Enumerating the HID devices is an expensive operation, so you can call this to see if there have been any system device changes since the last call to this function. A change in the counter returned doesn't necessarily mean that anything has changed, but you can call to get an updated device list.
Calling this function for the first time may cause a thread or other system resource to be allocated to track device change notifications.

member.returns: a change counter that is incremented with each potential device change, or 0 if device change detection isn't available.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDEnumerate(System.UInt16,System.UInt16)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_hid_device_info * SDLCALL SDL_hid_enumerate(unsigned short vendor_id, unsigned short product_id);

summary.para: Enumerate the HID Devices.

This function returns a linked list of all the HID devices attached to the system which match vendorId and productId. If vendorId is set to 0 then any vendor matches. If productId is set to 0 then any product matches. If vendorId and productId are both set to 0, then all HID devices will be returned.
By default SDL will only enumerate controllers, to reduce risk of hanging or crashing on bad drivers, but can be set to "0" to enumerate all HID devices.

member.param: the Vendor ID (VID) of the types of device to open, or 0 to match any vendor.
the Product ID (PID) of the types of device to open, or 0 to match any product.
member.returns: a pointer to a linked list of type , containing information about the HID devices attached to the system, or NULL in the case of failure. Free this linked list by calling .

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDWrite(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[],System.UIntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_write(SDL_hid_device *dev, const unsigned char *data, size_t length);

summary.para: Write an Output report to a HID device.

The first byte of data must contain the Report ID. For devices which only support a single report, this must be set to 0x0. The remaining bytes contain the report data. Since the Report ID is mandatory, calls to will always contain one more byte than the report contains. For example, if a hid report is 16 bytes long, 17 bytes must be passed to , the Report ID (or 0x0, for devices with a single report), followed by the report data (16 bytes). In this example, the length passed in would be 17.
will send the data on the first OUT endpoint, if one exists. If it does not, it will send the data through the Control Endpoint (Endpoint 0).

member.param: a device handle returned from .
the data to send, including the report number as the first byte.
the length in bytes of the data to send.
member.returns: the actual number of bytes written and -1 on on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDReadTimeout(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[]@,System.UIntPtr,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_read_timeout(SDL_hid_device *dev, unsigned char *data, size_t length, int milliseconds);

summary.para: Read an Input report from a HID device with timeout.
Input reports are returned to the host through the INTERRUPT IN endpoint. The first byte will contain the Report number if the device uses numbered reports.

member.param: a device handle returned from .
a buffer to put the read data into.
the number of bytes to read. For devices with multiple reports, make sure to read an extra byte for the report number.
timeout in milliseconds or -1 for blocking wait.
member.returns: the actual number of bytes read and -1 on on failure; call for more information. If no packet was available to be read within the timeout period, this function returns 0.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDRead(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[]@,System.UIntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_read(SDL_hid_device *dev, unsigned char *data, size_t length);

summary.para: Read an Input report from a HID device.
Input reports are returned to the host through the INTERRUPT IN endpoint. The first byte will contain the Report number if the device uses numbered reports.

member.param: a device handle returned from .
a buffer to put the read data into.
the number of bytes to read. For devices with multiple reports, make sure to read an extra byte for the report number.
member.returns: the actual number of bytes read and -1 on failure; call for more information. If no packet was available to be read and the handle is in non-blocking mode, this function returns 0.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDSetNonBlocking(System.IntPtr,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_set_nonblocking(SDL_hid_device *dev, int nonblock);

summary.para: Set the device handle to be non-blocking.
In non-blocking mode calls to will return immediately with a value of 0 if there is no data to be read. In blocking mode, will wait (block) until there is data to read before returning.
Nonblocking can be turned on and off at any time.

member.param: a device handle returned from .
enable or not the nonblocking reads - 1 to enable nonblocking - 0 to disable nonblocking.
member.returns: 0 on success or a negative error code on failure; call for more information.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDSendFeatureReport(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[],System.UIntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_send_feature_report(SDL_hid_device *dev, const unsigned char *data, size_t length);

summary.para: Send a Feature report to the device.

Feature reports are sent over the Control endpoint as a Set_Report transfer. The first byte of data must contain the Report ID. For devices which only support a single report, this must be set to 0x0. The remaining bytes contain the report data. Since the Report ID is mandatory, calls to will always contain one more byte than the report contains. For example, if a hid report is 16 bytes long, 17 bytes must be passed to : the Report ID (or 0x0, for devices which do not use numbered reports), followed by the report data (16 bytes). In this example, the length passed in would be 17.

member.param: a device handle returned from .
the data to send, including the report number as the first byte.
the length in bytes of the data to send, including the report number.
member.returns: the actual number of bytes written and -1 on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HIDGetFeatureReport(System.IntPtr,System.Byte[]@,System.UIntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_hid_get_feature_report(SDL_hid_device *dev, unsigned char *data, size_t length);

summary.para: Get a feature report from a HID device.

Set the first byte of data to the Report ID of the report to be read. Make sure to allow space for this extra byte in data. Upon return, the first byte will still contain the Report ID, and the report data will start in data[1].

member.param: a device handle returned from .
a buffer to put the read data into, including the Report ID. Set the first byte of data to the Report ID of the report to be read, or set it to zero if your device does not use numbered reports.
the number of bytes to read, including an extra byte for the report ID. The buffer can be longer than the actual report.
member.returns: the number of bytes read plus one for the report ID (which is still in the first byte), or -1 on on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

Set the first byte of data to the Report ID of the report to be read. Make sure to allow space for this extra byte in data. Upon return, the first byte will still contain the Report ID, and the report data will start in data[1].

member.param: a device handle returned from .
a buffer to put the read data into, including the Report ID. Set the first byte of data to the Report ID of the report to be read, or set it to zero if your device does not use numbered reports.
the number of bytes to read, including an extra byte for the report ID. The buffer can be longer than the actual report.
member.returns: the number of bytes read plus one for the report ID (which is still in the first byte), or -1 on on failure; call for more information.

summary.para: An enum of some common joystick types.
This is by no means a complete list of everything that can be plugged into a computer.
You may refer to XInput Controller Types table for a general understanding of each joystick type.

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.LockJoysticks
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_LockJoysticks(void) SDL_ACQUIRE(SDL_joystick_lock);

summary.para: Locking for atomic access to the joystick API.
The SDL joystick functions are thread-safe, however you can lock the joysticks while processing to guarantee that the joystick list won't change and joystick and gamepad events will not be delivered.

member.threadsafety: This should be called from the same thread that called .
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.TryLockJoysticks
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_TryLockJoysticks(void) SDL_ACQUIRE(SDL_joystick_lock);

summary.para: Locking for atomic access to the joystick API.
The SDL joystick functions are thread-safe, however you can lock the joysticks while processing to guarantee that the joystick list won't change and joystick and gamepad events will not be delivered.

member.returns: true if the joysticks were successfully locked, false otherwise.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.6.0.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.UnlockJoysticks
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_UnlockJoysticks(void) SDL_RELEASE(SDL_joystick_lock);
member.summary: Unlocking for atomic access to the joystick API.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.AttachVirtualJoystick(SDL3.SDL.VirtualJoystickDesc@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_JoystickID SDLCALL SDL_AttachVirtualJoystick(const SDL_VirtualJoystickDesc *desc);
member.summary:
Attach a new virtual joystick.
Apps can create virtual joysticks, that exist without hardware directly backing them, and have program-supplied inputs. Once attached, a virtual joystick looks like any other joystick that SDL can access. These can be used to make other things look like joysticks, or provide pre-recorded input, etc.
Once attached, the app can send joystick inputs to the new virtual joystick using , etc.
When no longer needed, the virtual joystick can be removed by calling .
member.param: joystick description, initialized using .
member.returns: the joystick instance ID, or 0 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

summary.para: Set the state of an axis on an opened virtual joystick.

Please note that values set here will not be applied until the next call to , which can either be called directly, or can be called indirectly through various other SDL APIs, including, but not limited to the following: , , , .
Note that when sending trigger axes, you should scale the value to the full range of Sint16. For example, a trigger at rest would have the value of .

member.param: the virtual joystick on which to set state.
the index of the axis on the virtual joystick to update.
the new value for the specified axis.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

This is called automatically by the event loop if any joystick events are enabled.

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetJoystickAxis(System.IntPtr,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC Sint16 SDLCALL SDL_GetJoystickAxis(SDL_Joystick *joystick, int axis);

summary.para: Get the current state of an axis control on a joystick.

SDL makes no promises about what part of the joystick any given axis refers to. Your game should have some sort of configuration UI to let users specify what each axis should be bound to. Alternately, SDL's higher-level Game Controller API makes a great effort to apply order to this lower-level interface, so you know that a specific axis is the "left thumb stick," etc.
The value returned by is a signed integer (-32768 to 32767) representing the current position of the axis. It may be necessary to impose certain tolerances on these values to account for jitter.

member.param: an SDL_Joystick structure containing joystick information.
the axis to query; the axis indices start at index 0.
member.returns: a 16-bit signed integer representing the current position of the axis or 0 on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetJoystickAxisInitialState(System.IntPtr,System.Int32,System.Int16@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_GetJoystickAxisInitialState(SDL_Joystick *joystick, int axis, Sint16 *state);

summary.para: Get the initial state of an axis control on a joystick.
The state is a value ranging from -32768 to 32767.
The axis indices start at index 0.

member.param: an SDL_Joystick structure containing joystick information.
the axis to query; the axis indices start at index 0.
upon return, the initial value is supplied here.
member.returns: true if this axis has any initial value, or false if not.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetJoystickBall(System.IntPtr,System.Int32,System.Int32@,System.Int32@)

summary.para: Get the ball axis change since the last poll.
Trackballs can only return relative motion since the last call to , these motion deltas are placed into dx and dy.
Most joysticks do not have trackballs.

member.param: the SDL_Joystick to query.
the ball index to query; ball indices start at index 0.
stores the difference in the x axis position since the last poll.
stores the difference in the y axis position since the last poll.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RumbleJoystick(System.IntPtr,System.Int16,System.Int16,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_RumbleJoystick(SDL_Joystick *joystick, Uint16 low_frequency_rumble, Uint16 high_frequency_rumble, Uint32 duration_ms);

summary.para: Start a rumble effect.
Each call to this function cancels any previous rumble effect, and calling it with 0 intensity stops any rumbling.
This function requires you to process SDL events or call to update rumble state.

member.param: the joystick to vibrate.
the intensity of the low frequency (left) rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the intensity of the high frequency (right) rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the duration of the rumble effect, in milliseconds.
member.returns: true, or false if rumble isn't supported on this joystick.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.RumbleJoystickTriggers(System.IntPtr,System.Int16,System.Int16,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_RumbleJoystickTriggers(SDL_Joystick *joystick, Uint16 left_rumble, Uint16 right_rumble, Uint32 duration_ms);

summary.para: Start a rumble effect in the joystick's triggers.
Each call to this function cancels any previous trigger rumble effect, and calling it with 0 intensity stops any rumbling.

Note that this is rumbling of the _triggers_ and not the game controller as a whole. This is currently only supported on Xbox One controllers. If you want the (more common) whole-controller rumble, use instead.
This function requires you to process SDL events or call to update rumble state.

member.param: the joystick to vibrate.
the intensity of the left trigger rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the intensity of the right trigger rumble motor, from 0 to 0xFFFF.
the duration of the rumble effect, in milliseconds.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

An example of a joystick LED is the light on the back of a PlayStation 4's DualShock 4 controller.
For joysticks with a single color LED, the maximum of the RGB values will be used as the LED brightness.

the intensity of the green LED.
the intensity of the blue LED.

member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetJoystickPowerInfo(System.IntPtr,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_PowerState SDLCALL SDL_GetJoystickPowerInfo(SDL_Joystick *joystick, int *percent);

summary.para: Get the battery state of a joystick.

You should never take a battery status as absolute truth. Batteries (especially failing batteries) are delicate hardware, and the values reported here are best estimates based on what that hardware reports. It's not uncommon for older batteries to lose stored power much faster than it reports, or completely drain when reporting it has 20 percent left, etc.

member.param: the joystick to query.
a pointer filled in with the percentage of battery life left, between 0 and 100, or null to ignore. This will be filled in with -1 we can't determine a value or there is no battery.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.returns: the current battery state or on failure; call for more information.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetKeyboardState(System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const bool * SDLCALL SDL_GetKeyboardState(int *numkeys);

summary.para: Get a snapshot of the current state of the keyboard.
The pointer returned is a pointer to an internal SDL array. It will be valid for the whole lifetime of the application and should not be freed by the caller.
A array element with a value of true means that the key is pressed and a value of false means that it is not. Indexes into this array are obtained by using values.
Use to update the state array.

This function gives you the current state after all events have been processed, so if a key or button has been pressed and released before you process events, then the pressed state will never show up in the calls.
Note: This function doesn't take into account whether shift has been pressed or not.

member.param: if non-NULL, receives the length of the returned array.
member.returns: a pointer to an array of key states.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

If you want to get the keycode as it would be delivered in key events, including options specified in , then you should pass keyEvent as true. Otherwise this function simply translates the scancode based on the given modifier state.

member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC const char * SDLCALL SDL_GetScancodeName(SDL_Scancode scancode);

summary.para: Get a human-readable name for a scancode.

Warning: The returned name is by design not stable across platforms, e.g. the name for is "Left GUI" under Linux but "Left Windows" under Microsoft Windows, and some scancodes like don't have any name at all. There are even scancodes that share names, e.g. and (both called "Return"). This function is therefore unsuitable for creating a stable cross-platform two-way mapping between strings and scancodes.

member.param: the desired SDL_Scancode to query.
member.returns: a pointer to the name for the scancode. If the scancode doesn't have a name this function returns an empty string ("").
member.threadsafety: This function is not thread safe.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.StartTextInput(System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_StartTextInput(SDL_Window *window);

summary.para: Start accepting Unicode text input events in a window.
This function will enable text input ( and events) in the specified window. Please use this function paired with .
Text input events are not received by default.
On some platforms using this function shows the screen keyboard and/or activates an IME, which can prevent some key press events from being passed through.

member.param: the window to enable text input.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.StartTextInputWithProperties(System.IntPtr,System.UInt32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_StartTextInputWithProperties(SDL_Window *window, SDL_PropertiesID props);

summary.para: Start accepting Unicode text input events in a window, with properties describing the input.
This function will enable text input ( and events) in the specified window. Please use this function paired with .
Text input events are not received by default.
On some platforms using this function shows the screen keyboard and/or activates an IME, which can prevent some key press events from being passed through.
These are the supported properties:

list.item: - an value that describes text being input, defaults to .
- an value that describes how text should be capitalized, defaults to for normal text entry, for , and for e-mail addresses, usernames, and passwords.
- true to enable auto completion and auto correction, defaults to true.
- true if multiple lines of text are allowed. This defaults to true if is "0" or is not set, and defaults to false if is "1".

summary.para: On Android you can directly specify the input type:

list.item: - the text input type to use, overriding other properties. This is documented at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/text/InputType

member.param: the window to enable text input.
the properties to use.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.param: the window for which to set the text input area.
the representing the text input area, in window coordinates, or null to clear it.
the offset of the current cursor location relative to rect.x, in window coordinates.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetTextInputArea(System.IntPtr,SDL3.SDL.Rect@,System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_GetTextInputArea(SDL_Window *window, SDL_Rect *rect, int *cursor);

summary.para: Get the area used to type Unicode text input.
This returns the values previously set by .

member.param: the window for which to query the text input area.
a pointer to an filled in with the text input area, may be null.
a pointer to the offset of the current cursor location relative to rect->x, may be null.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.HasScreenKeyboardSupport
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_HasScreenKeyboardSupport(void);

summary.para: whether the platform has screen keyboard support.

member.returns: true if the platform has some screen keyboard support or false if not.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.ScreenKeyboardShown(System.IntPtr)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_ScreenKeyboardShown(SDL_Window *window);

summary.para: Check whether the screen keyboard is shown for given window.

member.param: the window for which screen keyboard should be queried.
member.returns: true if screen keyboard is shown or false if not.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.TextInputType

summary.para: Text input type.
These are the valid values for . Not every value is valid on every platform, but where a value isn't supported, a reasonable fallback will be used.

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.TextInputType.Text
member.summary: The input is text

member.summary: The input is a number

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.TextInputType.NumberPasswordVisible
member.summary: The input is a secure PIN that is visible

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.Keycode

summary.para: The SDL virtual key representation.

Values of this type are used to represent keyboard keys using the current layout of the keyboard. These values include Unicode values representing the unmodified character that would be generated by pressing the key, or an SDLK_* constant for those keys that do not generate characters.
A special exception is the number keys at the top of the keyboard which map to SDLK_0...SDLK_9 on AZERTY layouts.
Keys with the bit set do not map to a scancode or Unicode code point

member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.MouseMotionTransformCallback

summary.para: A callback used to transform mouse motion delta from raw values.
This is called during SDL's handling of platform mouse events to scale the values of the resulting motion delta.

member.param: what was passed as userdata to .
the associated time at which this mouse motion event was received.
the associated window to which this mouse motion event was addressed.
the associated mouse from which this mouse motion event was emitted.
pointer to a variable that will be treated as the resulting x-axis motion.
pointer to a variable that will be treated as the resulting y-axis motion.
member.threadsafety: This callback is called by SDL's internal mouse input processing procedure, which may be a thread separate from the main event loop that is run at realtime priority. Stalling this thread with too much work in the callback can therefore potentially freeze the entire system. Care should be taken with proper synchronization practices when adding other side effects beyond mutation of the x and y values.
member.since: This datatype is available since SDL 3.2.6.

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetMouseState(System.Single@,System.Single@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_MouseButtonFlags SDLCALL SDL_GetMouseState(float *x, float *y);

summary.para: Query SDL's cache for the synchronous mouse button state and the window-relative SDL-cursor position.
This function returns the cached synchronous state as SDL understands it from the last pump of the event queue.
To query the platform for immediate asynchronous state, use .

member.param: a pointer to receive the SDL-cursor's x-position from the focused window's top left corner, can be null if unused.
a pointer to receive the SDL-cursor's y-position from the focused window's top left corner, can be null if unused.
member.returns: a 32-bit bitmask of the button state that can be bitwise-compared against the macro.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetGlobalMouseState(System.Single@,System.Single@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_MouseButtonFlags SDLCALL SDL_GetGlobalMouseState(float *x, float *y);

summary.para: Query the platform for the asynchronous mouse button state and the desktop-relative platform-cursor position.
This function immediately queries the platform for the most recent asynchronous state, more costly than retrieving SDL's cached state in .
Passing non-NULL pointers to x or y will write the destination with respective x or y coordinates relative to the desktop.
In Relative Mode, the platform-cursor's position usually contradicts the SDL-cursor's position as manually calculated from and .

This function can be useful if you need to track the mouse outside of a specific window and doesn't fit your needs. For example, it could be useful if you need to track the mouse while dragging a window, where coordinates relative to a window might not be in sync at all times.

member.param: a pointer to receive the platform-cursor's x-position from the desktop's top left corner, can be null if unused.
a pointer to receive the platform-cursor's y-position from the desktop's top left corner, can be null if unused.
member.returns: a 32-bit bitmask of the button state that can be bitwise-compared against the macro.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetRelativeMouseState(System.Single@,System.Single@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_MouseButtonFlags SDLCALL SDL_GetRelativeMouseState(float *x, float *y);

summary.para: Query SDL's cache for the synchronous mouse button state and accumulated mouse delta since last call.
This function returns the cached synchronous state as SDL understands it from the last pump of the event queue.
To query the platform for immediate asynchronous state, use .
In Relative Mode, the platform-cursor's position usually contradicts the SDL-cursor's position as manually calculated from and .
Passing non-NULL pointers to x or y will write the destination with respective x or y deltas accumulated since the last call to this function (or since event initialization).

This function is useful for reducing overhead by processing relative mouse inputs in one go per-frame instead of individually per-event, at the expense of losing the order between events within the frame (e.g. quickly pressing and releasing a button within the same frame).

member.param: a pointer to receive the x mouse delta accumulated since last call, can be null if unused.
a pointer to receive the y mouse delta accumulated since last call, can be null if unused.
member.returns: a 32-bit bitmask of the button state that can be bitwise-compared against the macro.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.WarpMouseInWindow(System.IntPtr,System.Single,System.Single)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC void SDLCALL SDL_WarpMouseInWindow(SDL_Window * window, float x, float y);

summary.para: Move the mouse cursor to the given position within the window.
This function generates a mouse motion event if relative mode is not enabled. If relative mode is enabled, you can force mouse events for the warp by setting the hint.
Note that this function will appear to succeed, but not actually move the mouse when used over Microsoft Remote Desktop.

member.param: the window to move the mouse into, or null for the current mouse focus.
the x coordinate within the window.
the y coordinate within the window.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.SetWindowRelativeMouseMode(System.IntPtr,System.Boolean)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_SetWindowRelativeMouseMode(SDL_Window *window, bool enabled);

summary.para: Set relative mouse mode for a window.

While the window has focus and relative mouse mode is enabled, the cursor is hidden, the mouse position is constrained to the window, and SDL will report continuous relative mouse motion even if the mouse is at the edge of the window.

If you'd like to keep the mouse position fixed while in relative mode you can use . If you'd like the cursor to be at a specific location when relative mode ends, you should use before disabling relative mode.
This function will flush any pending mouse motion for this window.

member.param: the window to change.
true to enable relative mode, false to disable.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CaptureMouse(System.Boolean)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC bool SDLCALL SDL_CaptureMouse(bool enabled);

summary.para: Capture the mouse and to track input outside an SDL window.

Capturing enables your app to obtain mouse events globally, instead of just within your window. Not all video targets support this function. When capturing is enabled, the current window will get all mouse events, but unlike relative mode, no change is made to the cursor and it is not restrained to your window.

This function may also deny mouse input to other windows--both those in your application and others on the system--so you should use this function sparingly, and in small bursts. For example, you might want to track the mouse while the user is dragging something, until the user releases a mouse button. It is not recommended that you capture the mouse for long periods of time, such as the entire time your app is running. For that, you should probably use or , depending on your goals.

While captured, mouse events still report coordinates relative to the current (foreground) window, but those coordinates may be outside the bounds of the window (including negative values). Capturing is only allowed for the foreground window. If the window loses focus while capturing, the capture will be disabled automatically.
While capturing is enabled, the current window will have the flag set.

Please note that SDL will attempt to "auto capture" the mouse while the user is pressing a button; this is to try and make mouse behavior more consistent between platforms, and deal with the common case of a user dragging the mouse outside of the window. This means that if you are calling only to deal with this situation, you do not have to (although it is safe to do so). If this causes problems for your app, you can disable auto capture by setting the hint to zero.

member.param: true to enable capturing, false to disable.
member.returns: true on success or false on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CreateCursor(System.Byte[],System.Byte[],System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Cursor * SDLCALL SDL_CreateCursor(const Uint8 * data, const Uint8 * mask, int w, int h, int hot_x, int hot_y);

summary.para: Create a cursor using the specified bitmap data and mask (in MSB format).
mask has to be in MSB (Most Significant Bit) format.
The cursor width (w) must be a multiple of 8 bits.
The cursor is created in black and white according to the following:

list.item: data=0, mask=1: white
data=1, mask=1: black
data=0, mask=0: transparent
data=1, mask=0: inverted color if possible, black if not.

summary.para: Cursors created with this function must be freed with .

If you want to have a color cursor, or create your cursor from an SDL_Surface, you should use . Alternately, you can hide the cursor and draw your own as part of your game's rendering, but it will be bound to the framerate.
Also, is available, which provides several readily-available system cursors to pick from.

member.param: the color value for each pixel of the cursor.
the mask value for each pixel of the cursor.
the width of the cursor.
the height of the cursor.
the x-axis offset from the left of the cursor image to the mouse x position, in the range of 0 to w - 1.
the y-axis offset from the top of the cursor image to the mouse y position, in the range of 0 to h - 1.
member.returns: a new cursor with the specified parameters on success or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

members.member:
extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Cursor * SDLCALL SDL_CreateColorCursor(SDL_Surface *surface, int hot_x, int hot_y);
Create a color cursor.
If this function is passed a surface with alternate representations added with , the surface will be interpreted as the content to be used for 100% display scale, and the alternate representations will be used for high DPI situations if is enabled. For example, if the original surface is 32x32, then on a 2x macOS display or 200% display scale on Windows, a 64x64 version of the image will be used, if available. If a matching version of the image isn't available, the closest larger size image will be downscaled to the appropriate size and be used instead, if available. Otherwise, the closest smaller image will be upscaled and be used instead.
an structure representing the cursor image. the x position of the cursor hot spot. the y position of the cursor hot spot. the new cursor on success or null on failure; call for more information. This function should only be called on the main thread. This function is available since SDL 3.2.0+

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.CreateAnimatedCursor(SDL3.SDL.CursorFrameInfo[],System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_Cursor *SDLCALL SDL_CreateAnimatedCursor(SDL_CursorFrameInfo *frames,int frame_count, int hot_x, int hot_y);
member.summary:
Create an animated color cursor.
Animated cursors are composed of a sequential array of frames, specified as surfaces and durations in an array of structs. The hot spot coordinates are universal to all frames, and all frames must have the same dimensions.
Frame durations are specified in milliseconds. A duration of 0 implies an infinite frame time, and the animation will stop on that frame. To create a one-shot animation, set the duration of the last frame in the sequence to 0.
If this function is passed surfaces with alternate representations added with , the surfaces will be interpreted as the content to be used for 100% display scale, and the alternate representations will be used for high DPI situations. For example, if the original surfaces are 32x32, then on a 2x macOS display or 200% display scale on Windows, a 64x64 version of the image will be used, if available. If a matching version of the image isn't available, the closest larger size image will be downscaled to the appropriate size and be used instead, if available. Otherwise, the closest smaller image will be upscaled and be used instead.
If the underlying platform does not support animated cursors, this function will fall back to creating a static color cursor using the first frame in the sequence.
member.param: an array of cursor images composing the animation.
the number of frames in the sequence.
the x position of the cursor hot spot.
the y position of the cursor hot spot.
member.returns: the new cursor on success or null on failure; call for more information.
member.threadsafety: This function should only be called on the main thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.4.0.

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis

summary.para: Pen axis indices.

These are the valid values for the axis field in . All axes are either normalised to 0..1 or report a (positive or negative) angle in degrees, with 0.0 representing the centre. Not all pens/backends support all axes: unsupported axes are always zero.
To convert angles for tilt and rotation into vector representation, use SDL_sinf on the XTILT, YTILT, or ROTATION component, for example:

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.Pressure
member.summary: Pen pressure. Unidirectional: 0 to 1.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.XTilt
member.summary: Pen horizontal tilt angle. Bidirectional: -90.0 to 90.0 (left-to-right).

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.YTilt
member.summary: Pen vertical tilt angle. Bidirectional: -90.0 to 90.0 (top-to-down).

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.Distance
member.summary: Pen distance to drawing surface. Unidirectional: 0.0 to 1.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.Rotation
member.summary: Pen barrel rotation. Bidirectional: -180 to 179.9 (clockwise, 0 is facing up, -180.0 is facing down).

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.Slider
member.summary: Pen finger wheel or slider (e.g., Airbrush Pen). Unidirectional: 0 to 1.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.TangetialPressure
member.summary: Pressure from squeezing the pen ("barrel pressure").

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.PenAxis.Count
member.summary: Total known pen axis types in this version of SDL. This number may grow in future releases!

members.member:
An enum that describes the type of a pen device.
A device is a pen that touches a graphic display (like an Apple Pencil on an iPad's screen). devices touch an external tablet surface that is connected to the machine but is not a display (like a lower-end Wacom tablet connected over USB).
Apps may use this information to decide if they should draw a cursor; if the pen is touching the screen directly, a cursor doesn't make sense and can be in the way, but becomes necessary for indirect devices to know where on the display they are interacting.
/// This enum is available since SDL 3.4.0.

Get the device type of the given pen.

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.Scancode

summary.para: The SDL keyboard scancode representation.
An SDL scancode is the physical representation of a key on the keyboard, independent of language and keyboard mapping.
VValues of this type are used to represent keyboard keys, among other places in the scancode field of the structure.
The values in this enumeration are based on the USB usage page standard: https://usb.org/sites/default/files/hut1_5.pdf

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.Backslash
member.summary: Located at the lower left of the return key on ISO keyboards and at the right end of the QWERTY row on ANSI keyboards. Produces REVERSE SOLIDUS (backslash) and VERTICAL LINE in a US layout, REVERSE SOLIDUS and VERTICAL LINE in a UK Mac layout, NUMBER SIGN and TILDE in a UK Windows layout, DOLLAR SIGN and POUND SIGN in a Swiss German layout, NUMBER SIGN and APOSTROPHE in a German layout, GRAVE ACCENT and POUND SIGN in a French Mac layout, and ASTERISK and MICRO SIGN in a French Windows layout.

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.NonUshash
member.summary: ISO USB keyboards actually use this code instead of 49 for the same key, but all OSes I've seen treat the two codes identically. So, as an implementor, unless your keyboard generates both of those codes and your OS treats them differently, you should generate BACKSLASH instead of this code. As a user, you should not rely on this code because SDL will never generate it with most (all?) keyboards.

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.Grave
member.summary: Located in the top left corner (on both ANSI and ISO keyboards). Produces GRAVE ACCENT and TILDE in a US Windows layout and in US and UK Mac layouts on ANSI keyboards, GRAVE ACCENT and NOT SIGN in a UK Windows layout, SECTION SIGN and PLUS-MINUS SIGN in US and UK Mac layouts on ISO keyboards, SECTION SIGN and DEGREE SIGN in a Swiss German layout (Mac: only on ISO keyboards), CIRCUMFLEX ACCENT and DEGREE SIGN in a German layout (Mac: only on ISO keyboards), SUPERSCRIPT TWO and TILDE in a French Windows layout, COMMERCIAL AT and NUMBER SIGN in a French Mac layout on ISO keyboards, and LESS-THAN SIGN and GREATER-THAN SIGN in a Swiss German, German, or French Mac layout on ANSI keyboards.

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.Insert
member.summary: insert on PC, help on some Mac keyboards (but does send code 73, not 117)

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.NumLockClear
member.summary: num lock on PC, clear on Mac keyboards

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.NonUsBackSlash
member.summary: This is the additional key that ISO keyboards have over ANSI ones, located between left shift and Z. Produces GRAVE ACCENT and TILDE in a US or UK Mac layout, REVERSE SOLIDUS (backslash) and VERTICAL LINE in a US or UK Windows layout, and LESS-THAN SIGN and GREATER-THAN SIGN in a Swiss German, German, or French layout.

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.Application
member.summary: windows contextual menu, compose

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Scancode.Power
member.summary: The USB document says this is a status flag, not a physical key - but some Mac keyboards do have a power key.

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.StandardGravity

summary.para: A constant to represent standard gravity for accelerometer sensors.

The accelerometer returns the current acceleration in SI meters per second squared. This measurement includes the force of gravity, so a device at rest will have an value of SDL_STANDARD_GRAVITY away from the center of the earth, which is a positive Y value.

member.since: This macro is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.SensorType

summary.para: The different sensors defined by SDL.
Additional sensors may be available, using platform dependent semantics.
Here are the additional Android sensors:
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/hardware/SensorEvent.html#values
Accelerometer sensor notes:

The accelerometer returns the current acceleration in SI meters per second squared. This measurement includes the force of gravity, so a device at rest will have an value of away from the center of the earth, which is a positive Y value.

list.item: values[0]: Acceleration on the x axis
values[1]: Acceleration on the y axis
values[2]: Acceleration on the z axis

summary.para: For phones and tablets held in natural orientation and game controllers held in front of you, the axes are defined as follows:

list.item: -X ... +X : left ... right
-Y ... +Y : bottom ... top
-Z ... +Z : farther ... closer

summary.para: The accelerometer axis data is not changed when the device is rotated.
Gyroscope sensor notes:

The gyroscope returns the current rate of rotation in radians per second. The rotation is positive in the counter-clockwise direction. That is, an observer looking from a positive location on one of the axes would see positive rotation on that axis when it appeared to be rotating counter-clockwise.

list.item: values[0]: Angular speed around the x axis (pitch)
values[1]: Angular speed around the y axis (yaw)
values[2]: Angular speed around the z axis (roll)

summary.para: For phones and tablets held in natural orientation and game controllers held in front of you, the axes are defined as follows:

list.item: -X ... +X : left ... right
-Y ... +Y : bottom ... top
-Z ... +Z : farther ... closer

summary.para: The gyroscope axis data is not changed when the device is rotated.

member.since: This enum is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: T:SDL3.SDL.Finger

summary.para: Data about a single finger in a multitouch event.

Each touch event is a collection of fingers that are simultaneously in contact with the touch device (so a "touch" can be a "multitouch," in reality), and this struct reports details of the specific fingers.

member.since: This struct is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.summary: the x-axis location of the touch event, normalized (0...1)

member.summary: the y-axis location of the touch event, normalized (0...1)

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.Finger.Pressure
member.summary: the quantity of pressure applied, normalized (0...1)

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetTouchDevices(System.Int32@)
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC SDL_TouchID * SDLCALL SDL_GetTouchDevices(int *count);

summary.para: Get a list of registered touch devices.

On some platforms SDL first sees the touch device if it was actually used. Therefore the returned list might be empty, although devices are available. After using all devices at least once the number will be correct.

member.param: a pointer filled in with the number of devices returned, may be null.
member.returns: a 0 terminated array of touch device IDs or null on failure; call for more information. This should be freed with when it is no longer needed.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: F:SDL3.SDL.CacheLineSize
member.code: #define SDL_CACHELINE_SIZE 128

summary.para: A guess for the cacheline size used for padding.
Most x86 processors have a 64 byte cache line. The 64-bit PowerPC processors have a 128 byte cache line. We use the larger value to be generally safe.

member.since: This macro is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetNumLogicalCPUCores
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetNumLogicalCPUCores(void);
member.summary: Get the number of logical CPU cores available.
member.returns: the total number of logical CPU cores. On CPUs that include technologies such as hyperthreading, the number of logical cores may be more than the number of physical cores.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0

member.name: M:SDL3.SDL.GetCPUCacheLineSize
member.code: extern SDL_DECLSPEC int SDLCALL SDL_GetCPUCacheLineSize(void);

summary.para: Determine the L1 cache line size of the CPU.
This is useful for determining multi-threaded structure padding or SIMD prefetch sizes.

member.returns: the L1 cache line size of the CPU, in bytes.
member.threadsafety: It is safe to call this function from any thread.
member.since: This function is available since SDL 3.2.0
